---
title: "Essential Digital Skills"
subtitle: "Foundation pathway for confident, defensible digital research practice"
---

::: {.tier-card}
### Course At A Glance

- **Level:** Foundation
- **Best for:** Early-career researchers, research staff, and professional services colleagues
- **Main themes:** Collaboration, delivery, governance, research data management, data quality, interpretation, and responsible AI use
- **Format:** Core lesson sequence with AI extension sessions that can be used to deepen or tailor delivery
- **Outcome:** Better day-to-day judgement about where work should live, how it should be documented, and how research decisions can be made more safely and transparently
:::

::: {.two-col-grid}
::: {.item}
## Why This Course Matters

Modern research is full of small digital decisions that carry real consequences. Where should project files live? What counts as good enough documentation? When does a governance issue need escalation? How do you tell the difference between an interesting chart and an actionable insight?

Essential Digital Skills is designed to make those decisions easier. It gives learners a practical foundation for working inside the university research ecosystem with more confidence, less guesswork, and stronger habits for collaboration, compliance, and evidence.
:::

::: {.item}
## What You Will Actually Do

This is not a passive overview course. Learners work with realistic scenarios, map real workflows, critique weak practices, and improve them.

Across the course you will:

- map research tasks to the right institutional systems
- think through delivery and coordination problems
- identify governance and data protection risks
- improve data management and folder structures
- question data quality, charts, and interpretation
- explore what responsible and useful AI support looks like in research
:::
:::

## Who This Is For

This course is especially useful if you:

- are starting to work more seriously with digital research workflows
- want to feel more confident using university platforms and support systems
- need stronger habits for documentation, storage, sharing, and team coordination
- regularly make decisions about data, evidence, or reporting but have never had formal training in these areas

It is also a strong entry point for researchers who plan to continue into the Methods for Data Science pathway.

## You Will Build Confidence In

::: {.four-col-grid}
::: {.item}
### Working Better Together

Use the right tools for shared work, understand how systems connect, and reduce friction in collaboration.
:::

::: {.item}
### Managing Risk

Recognise when data, ethics, policy, or security issues need a more careful response.
:::

::: {.item}
### Making Data More Usable

Improve structure, documentation, metadata, and reuse potential rather than treating data management as an afterthought.
:::

::: {.item}
### Turning Information Into Action

Read charts more critically, add context more deliberately, and communicate insights more effectively.
:::
:::

## Learning Journey

| Lesson | What you will tackle | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| [01 Digital Tools](episodes/01-digital_tools.qmd) | Institutional systems, collaboration platforms, researcher identity, and digital security | Helps you understand the digital environment you are already working inside |
| [02 Digital Delivery](episodes/02-digital_delivery.qmd) | Planning, coordination, sprint thinking, and shared ownership | Gives teams a more structured way to move work forward |
| [03 Data Governance and Policy](episodes/03-data_governance_and_policy.qmd) | GDPR, ethics, classification, and compliance | Strengthens judgement around lawful, ethical, and institutionally defensible practice |
| [04 Research Data Management](episodes/04-research_data_managment.qmd) | DMPs, storage, metadata, archiving, and FAIR reuse | Turns RDM from paperwork into practical workflow design |
| [05 Collecting the Right Data](episodes/05-collecting_the_right_data.qmd) | Data sources, question design, formats, and fairness | Improves data quality before analysis even begins |
| [06 Organising and Exploring Data](episodes/06-organising_and_exploring_data.qmd) | Tidy structures, exploration, pattern finding, and early hypothesis building | Makes messy data easier to interpret and use |
| [07 Making Sense of Data](episodes/07-making_sense_of_data.qmd) | Visualisation, chart critique, interpretation, and context | Helps learners avoid overclaiming and misreading evidence |
| [08 Insight to Impact](episodes/08-insight_to_impacts.qmd) | Action, audience, feedback, and implementation | Connects analysis to decisions, communication, and change |

## AI Extension Sessions

The AI sessions extend the foundation course without losing the same practical emphasis on judgement, evidence, and accountability.

| Extension | Focus |
|---|---|
| [EXT1 How AI Impacts Research](episodes/ext1-how_ai_impacts_research.qmd) | Risks, governance, integrity, and the broader research context |
| [EXT2 How to Use AI in Research](episodes/ext2-how_to_use_ai_in_research.qmd) | Prompt design, source-bounded workflows, validation habits, and practical experimentation |

## What Learners Typically Leave With

By the end of the pathway, learners should be more able to:

- choose better tools and storage locations for their work
- identify weak points in project coordination and documentation
- ask sharper questions about data sensitivity, sharing, and governance
- improve folder structures, naming, and metadata for collaboration
- interpret data outputs more critically and communicate them more clearly
- use AI more selectively, with stronger boundaries and better verification

## How To Prepare

You do not need programming experience for this course. It helps to arrive with:

- access to your university account and core platforms such as M365, Teams, OneDrive, and SharePoint
- a device suitable for group exercises and practical tasks
- one current project or workflow in mind that you can use as a live example

Detailed preparation guidance is available in [Setup](learners/setup.qmd).

## Course Materials

::: {.two-col-grid}
::: {.item}
### Learners

- [Setup](learners/setup.qmd): what to bring, what to access, and how to prepare
- [Reference](learners/reference.qmd): glossary, platform guide, and practical checklists
- [Discussion](learners/discuss.qmd): prompts for breakout work and end-of-session reflection
:::

::: {.item}
### Instructors

- [Instructor Notes](instructors/instructor-notes.qmd): teaching focus, facilitation moves, and common sticking points
- [Additional Material](instructors/additional_material.qmd): optional extensions, demos, and case ideas
- [Extra Exercises](instructors/extra_exercises.qmd): backup activities for consolidation or deeper discussion
:::
:::

## Community and Course Information

- [Course README](README.qmd)
- [Contributing](CONTRIBUTING.qmd)
- [Learner Profiles](profiles/learner-profiles.qmd)
- [Code of Conduct](CODE_OF_CONDUCT.qmd)
- [License](LICENSE.qmd)
